Her BMI is 22.3, which is just slightly above the normal weight range (usually 18.5-25 is normal).

On examination, she has normal breast development.
That's interesting because sometimes in cases of disorders like Turner syndrome, breast development can be delayed or absent.
But here, her breasts are normal, so that might point away from Turner syndrome.
She has scant axillary hair.
Usually, in cases of hypothyroidism or Turner syndrome, there can be decreased axillary hair.
But again, she has some, so maybe not the most indicative sign.
The abdominal examination shows a firm, nontender left inguinal mass.
That's a key finding.

Wait, if the ultrasound didn't show a uterus or ovaries, that suggests that she might have a congenital absence of these structures.
But she's 17, so maybe she has a condition where she didn't develop these organs.
The blind vaginal pouch could be due to the absence of the uterus, leading to the vaginal canal being closed off, hence the pouch.
The lack of a uterus and ovaries points towards a condition called Mayer-Vicker's syndrome.
I remember that Mayer-Vicker's is a type of congenital disorder where the female doesn't have a uterus, ovaries, or fallopian tubes.
It's a rare condition, and these girls typically present with primary amenorrhea, which is what this patient has.
Also, the inguinal mass could be due to an inguinal hernia, but the fact that the mass is firm and nontender makes me think it's more structural, like a congenital absence leading to a blind pouch.
In Mayer-Vicker's, sometimes there's an inguinal hernia because the abdominal muscles aren't pulling in, which can cause an inguinal mass.
Putting it all together: no uterus, no ovaries, blind vaginal pouch, and primary amenorrhea.
Mayer-Vicker's syndrome fits all these findings.
It's a rare condition, but the absence of reproductive organs explains the lack of menarche and the other physical signs.

On examination, she has normal breast development, which is interesting because sometimes breast development can be delayed, but here it's normal.
She has scant axillary hair, which might suggest some delay in puberty, but not necessarily.
The abdominal exam shows a firm, nontender left inguinal mass.
That's a bit concerning.
Inguinal masses can be hernias, but given the other findings, maybe something else.
The pelvic exam shows a blind vaginal pouch.
A blind pouch usually means that the vagina is present but doesn't lead to the cervix, which could indicate a problem with the development of the reproductive tract.
Also, the ultrasound didn't show a uterus or ovaries.
That's a big clue.
If the uterus and ovaries aren't visualized, it suggests that they might be absent or malformed.
Putting this together, the absence of the uterus and ovaries on ultrasound points towards a congenital anomaly.
One possibility is Mayer-Rokitansky-Küster-Hauser (MRKH) syndrome.
MRKH is characterized by underdevelopment of the uterus and vagina, often leading to a blind vaginal pouch.

Women with MRKH typically have normal breast and ovarian development, which aligns with the patient's breast development and the presence of ovaries (though in this case, the ovaries weren't seen on ultrasound, which might be due to the extent of the anomaly).
Another possibility is androgen insensitivity syndrome, but that usually presents with other symptoms like infertility and underdeveloped external genitalia, which isn't the case here.
Also, the inguinal mass could be a hernia, but combined with the pelvic findings, it's more likely related to the reproductive system.
So, considering all these factors—delayed menarche, blind vaginal pouch, no uterus or ovaries on ultrasound—the most likely diagnosis is Mayer-Rokitansky-Küster-Hauser syndrome.
